item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is this so highly rated? The skip's a total garbage fire.

eh? eh?

Sorry…
I'll stop now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My main question is why burn the idol in the first place? Did they just not want to make the room for it or what?

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's licence (CC-BY-SA 3.0) and as such has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a great skip all-around. You hook me in the SCProcedures, and you keep me interested throughout. This also provides a solid explanation as to why the Anomalous Items page explicitly says "stop incinerating stuff please."

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The description is good, but for some reason the Containment Procedures really make it for me. For too often there's tons of blackboxes and super complex cells. This is really straight forward, tels you who is in charge of it, and even offers blueprints if it needs to be moved or re-built. It helped me suspend my disbelief quite easily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this! Poor little minor-divinity-cum-trashfire. Can we call it brain damage when the entity in question likely lacked a brain to begin with? Numen-damaged, perhaps. It definitely strikes a (sym)pathetic chord. And I could totally see this guy in Spirited Away, but not in any derivative fashion. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never quit, scantron.

we love you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i am the specialest snowflake and if anyone says otherwise i Will Cry

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i usually downvote the wierd -something scps by principal since until i get to something that makes that make sense its just special snowflake stupidity, but this one was good enough for me to upvote despite that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cognitive dissonance used as a containment measure? I love it.

item.title: Image attribution
item.content:encoded:
The incinerator image is from here, "© EC (2008)". By their legal notice, so long as the source is acknowledged, reuse is permitted. Yay.

The idol image is apparently from an Ebay auction — the difficulty I had re-finding it makes me wonder how I got it in the first place. I'm sending a message to the guy running the auction (housecarl10) as we speak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Charming example of why SCP is Secure Contain Protect, not Smash Conflagrate Pulverize.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The wikipedia article is irritatingly vague, so I'm going to go with maybe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation accidently a tsukumogami?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, yeah, I see what you mean. Gotcha

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would have to be at the end of the article for that to work, and I wanted the last line to be "Slated for incineration.".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, but is there any reason that the original image isn't in the same document as the main "file"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and a cart

BOW BEFORE ME, LOST SHOPPING CARTS OF THE WAL-MART PARKING LOT. I AM YOUR GOD.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: THE TRASH HEAP HAS SPOKEN.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this so cute to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A timed broadcast has a single error in transmission time because of the extra day of July in 1996 that is listed in the Log of Extranormal Events? As in, the broadcast is off by a day or an hour or something?

Did I understand that correctly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea how to parse what you just said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liking this. +1.

Edit: pretty sure the rest of this comment was supposed to go on a different page and wound up here by mistake. Silly past me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish I could +1 posts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find it kinda amusing that they're basically trying to get this god to convert to Buddhism.

New Employee Orientation
"Okay, and this is where we keep the Buddhist god."
"Wait, I thought Buddhism didn't have gods."
"It doesn't. This is a god that believes in Buddhism."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Who the hell has 1902 as their favorite number?)

(But thanks)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Crono and Petra have the right idea here. Key word is original documentation — the anomalous item write-up you see there is the first stuff they wrote down on it. Presumably it's since been changed to reflect reality, but at one point in time, they did plan to incinerate that idol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

That's a note from the past. They burned an idol that was an anomalous item. The idol just shook, but it /did/ contain something that was a minor deity or about that power level. The deity itself couldn't be destroyed, but the fire gave it brain damage, so when it incarnated in the burning garbage after being forcibly deincarnated from the idol, it had brain damage and couldn't remember things for very long, keep a consistent personality, or distinguish humans from dogs, robots, or wind-up toys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe the idea is that the idol WAS incinerated, and that's why there's an SCP now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For my favorite number, I didn't expect it to become a literal pile of garbage.
Anyway, this is absolutely wonderful. The build of it is great and the little twist is pretty good.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uh, guys? did anyone else notice that the idol is slated for incineration?

Derp much?

Unless someone wants to explain what's going on there, but to me, putting something that was in a pile of trash that doesn't want to get burned just seems like a bad idea

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My concept of the reason for its variable nature is that it's got the deity equivalent of brain damage, but I like your interpretation too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the idea that anomalous doesn't necessarily go away easily. To me this SCP really sells why the foundation does not destroy everything it comes across. I have a question though, does the SCP think that the recordings are like a sort of prayer chant from it's followers, and so curbs its baser urges to fit it's desire for worship instead? If so I really like the idea of psychological warfare that is being used.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kids, this and that one chair the GOC messed with are the reasons why you should let mobile wood alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That final addendum absolutely sold this for me. It's really cool seeing an underrated part of the site get a mention like that, and while I initially wasn't fond of the idea, the addendum pulls it together in a way that brings it from okay to fantastic.

It also tickled me because of how often you mess around with the Log of Anomalous Items; kind of a neat callback to yourself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dang, whatta twist. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The article as a whole is really good, but I wanted to praise this sentence in the containment procedures in particular:

The recordings must consist primarily of speech in which the speaker praises or argues in favor of values including moderation, weight loss, humility, and pacifism.

It's just odd and specific enough to draw the reader's attention from the very beginning. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn it, Foundation! Stop making gods out of garbage! Pop culture does enough of it as is!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder how long it took them to figure it out.

"Guys, it came out of an incinerator and it can Katamari burnt things. I don't think setting it on fire will w-" "Shut up and get more gasoline."

But yeah, definitely not the response I was expecting.

Easter egg: Dr. Hall is actually my (head)canon's version of Iceberg.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The best part is that for once, fire is not the answer. Considering how often fire is the most (or only) effective solution, this tickles me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really absolutely love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just… love this. Especially the sting at the end. And the fact that yeah, the incineration of the presumably-harmless probably-at-least-a-little-divine idol backfired, but not in an 'AND THEN THERE WAS A HORRIBLE MONSTER THAT HATED EVERYONE FOREVER' way. I mean, yeah, a massive sapient pile of burning garbage is a pain to contain, but they're managing pretty well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't got a lot to say about this one. tl;dr you sit in an idol for long enough and then it gets burned, you're not gonna come out in one piece.
